item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As a fan of short articles, this is exactly what I want out of them. It has an interesting premise and explores it without overstaying its welcome. +1

item.title: Interesting
item.content:encoded: Very Interesting, I don't seem to be the only one who thinks that i think you just got a GREAT scp dude great job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Minimal skips are tricky pull off, especially when they rely on fairly intricate concepts like this one. When done poorly, they tend to feel like they should be labeled "Some Assembly Required"; without a demonstration of the concept, the reader's imagination has to do the bulk of the heavy lifting to make the article interesting.

When done well, as I think you've done, the base concept feels like a fully-formed playground for the reader's imagination to romp in. You also have a concept that invites the reader to project their own experiences and feelings onto it, which dovetails perfectly into the themes at play, which is quite elegant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I’m glad you liked it, thanks for the typo fix!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was short and sweet. A very emotional piece. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's fair. It's not a huge deal, I just figured I'd over a bit of personal crit very much based on my own tastes. I still very much enjoyed it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
just because we masquerade as an archive of technical documents doesn't mean we need to write like one.

There's a reason why I still +1'd it :]

I have no real qualms with it, but it's still something I think could have contributed some to it. But it did not cause me to dislike the SCP at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Short, but meaningful. I enjoyed this a lot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Caro was really unsure about the sketch so I’ll be sure to pass that sentiment along :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There were a lot of things like that which were left on the cutting room floor, my goal was to make this no longer than around four paragraphs so any ancillary information not directly furthering the story got left out. If it helps square your head canon I usually imagine short SCPs to be the documentation accessible to someone with a very low security clearance.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
just because we masquerade as an archive of technical documents doesn't mean we need to write like one. the best scips use technical writing's ability to hide, not tell: through brief snapshots and terse notes on a world almost like our own, we construct beautiful possibilities. nobody reads the methodology section anyway.

anyway this article does exactly that, and the illustration goes a long way in setting the mood. easy +1 to Roget.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very excellent, I enjoyed this quite a bit. Leaves a bunch of questions at the end but I do not mind that, really, with some exception.

If I were to have one critique, which is a critique I often have for very short scips like this, it feels as if there is not much "research" included in this that I would expect from the Foundation. For instance, did the Foundation go approach the genetic testing company that these letters came from? Did they go to the return address? Did they witness an occurrence of this phenomenon? How did they find out about it at all?

These are questions I feel like are left unanswered that I, personally, do not believe the Foundation would leave either unanswered or, at least, addressed to some degree.

Otherwise, this is very poignant as a little parable. Perhaps I should go plan a family reunion. :] +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Beautiful, lightweight, creative, delicate. I definitely want to see more things like this. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is neat. What's interesting here is that the article doesn't really pass judgment on anything - it doesn't decree that the things that are causing people distress are good or bad, just that they might be causing them distress. This isn't unreasonable, I think - even people who make difficult decisions for their own good or the good of others might feel lingering guilt or uncertainty about them.

The bit about reconciliation at the end is a little weak, but I don't think it's out of place. People who are suffering often reach out to those closest to them for support. Having to face the problems that cause them grief would obviously trigger a similar response, to the point where I'm not even sure that part is anomalous.

Pretty good. For an article as thin as this one is, there's a lot to think about here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Coldposted, apart from my fiancee Caroline providing proofreading, key brainstorming, and the image. Hope you guys enjoy. This was inspired by a snippet of conversation I overheard leaving class tonight and the idea demanded it be written.

Hope you enjoy.
